question_text: An electromagnetic wave and a mechanical wave both have a frequency of 100 Hz. In a vacuum, the electromagnetic wave travels at 3 × 10⁸ m/s. Which statement best explains why these two waves cannot have the same wavelength?

options.label: A
options.text: Mechanical waves require a medium and travel much slower than electromagnetic waves in a vacuum

options.label: B
options.text: Electromagnetic waves always have a higher frequency than mechanical waves

options.label: C
options.text: The wavelength of electromagnetic waves cannot be calculated using v = f × λ

options.label: D
options.text: Mechanical waves have a larger amplitude, so their wavelength must be larger

explanation: Since v = f × λ, if both waves have the same frequency (100 Hz) but travel at vastly different speeds (3 × 10⁸ m/s for light versus perhaps 340 m/s for sound), they must have proportionally different wavelengths. The electromagnetic wave's much higher speed means it has a much longer wavelength for the same frequency.
